I think there's two questions there. So the first one is about our D client that we have did a kitchen for over 12 months ago before we could screen out clients.

And this kitchen that we put in, it wasn't a full kitchen so it was leaving a stone bench top, replacing all the kitchen cupboards with new hardware, putting in not the. The carcass but putting in like new doors, new drawer runners and everything like that. We did a new splashback.
We put new above cabinetry, cabinetry in and also splash backed in there. What is it? Walk in pantry. Anyway, during the job some of the hinges were not in the right place.

So Brad had to get some doors resprayed for free because we were so late giving him the proposal for the works. We were kind and took a couple hundred dollars off. Even when we did the kitchen, Brad got accused of damaging the floor even though he had all the flooring covers down and dust protectors and that down.

So he ripped up floorboards from the kitchen through to the dining room into the study and replaced them through there. Anyway, they've come back to us again because some of the cupboards are scratching on the side when they.

Because they've got an internal hubbard so they've got a cupboard that opens up and then drawers which they're not opening up their drawers enough. And it's scratching on the internal doors so they want them free sprayed.
And he's also going back out there once before to like retighten up some hinges because they were just catching a little bit.

He went out there on Friday with the manufacturer. The manufacturer said that we haven't installed anything wrong. It's all fine. There was one little thing, maybe, possibly, but he said like it's still within their lenience and so he's happy that that's still covered. But he was just saying that we.

A solution could be just to respray the doors but to get them to sign something to say that they're happy and they're not going to come back at us with anything. And they've also got brand new floorboards through their house.
So what's the question, Tara?

How do I get them to sign something or say that they're. Because even when they finished the kitchen I had to make up a form to say that they were happy with the Kitchen and how it was. How do I get them to not come back again for something else?

That's a great question. So I'm going to summarize the question in a different way just so I try and make it more relevant for everyone on the call. We're basically the question is how do you put a boundary in place with a D class client?

We all have D class clients and there is always times in which we're going to have to put some firm boundaries in place. Tara. I think it's being really explicit in the information that we see and then we ask for confirmation of comprehension.

So when we go back to this client, you might step out exactly what we're going to do and then we put a disclaimer in. Any further damage will be blah, blah, blah at the cost of the client. Once this is completed, you acknowledge that.

So here's the key statement. You acknowledge that works have complete been completed above and beyond work. What's required. Again, you're going to have to summarize and do a better job of language with some time and consideration. But it's that we acknowledge that that you need to put in there.

You put your firm boundary in place and then that's it. The next key is if they come back after that, which D class clients often will, it's holding firm that boundary and not engaging in a further conversation.

If you know that you have covered all of your statutory warranties, that you've gone above and beyond in what is necessary in completing the work and then fixing any issues that have come up, then you need to hold that boundary and do not engage in a conversation.
So holding the boundary looks really and simply like referring them back to the prior agreement and leaving it there. Which we can help you with if it happens.
Because I feel like I've sent them an email before when they wanted stuff done that it would occur the cost of Brad coming out to do works. But they're like, no, no.

This is where. This is probably the only advantage Queensland have with such crappy laws around our contracts in building. Our laws state that any works over $3,300 has to have a contract. And all of this is stipulated in the contract. What I am and I am not responsible for.

So I think that's basically what you're going to have to assert with this email or document that you send out to them. This is what I am required to do. This is what we're actually doing and this is the boundary we're holding moving forward.
Yeah, because I'm waiting for master builders to call me back too, just to double check what I have to cover. So that will.

That will absolutely nail it for you. If you don't have a contract, basically you don't have to warrant anything. That's the risk that they take. That's not great for your reputation. And I would suggest that you don't necessarily walk that line. And you're not.

You're taking accountability for over and be up over and beyond. I think at this point you refer back to the fact that you've had the team from Blum out, they've checked the installation, the installation has been passed, is fine.

This is what we're going to do. Just so that you end up with the quality that you're hopeful for. But this will be the end of our communication about these issues. It's just being really, really firm.

And then we want them to reply with confirmation that they understand our terms moving forward. So the. By replying they're saying, yes, we accept. If they don't reply, then we don't do the works until we get some form of acceptance.
Again, there's a mini boundary to hold before the bigger boundary to hold. Yeah. Cool. Okay. Was there a second question?
Is there a second question on there? I thought I asked one.

So your other question was talking about how long it's going to take and then. And how frustrated the client was. And then you're talking here about somebody's asking for a timeline or maybe it's just a different job.

And so now it's about seven weeks worth of work. And it's. We've been talking to them, I think now for nearly six months to get this job kind of looked at locked in. We did an initial $500 deposit for drawings and things like that, but it just gone back there a few times now.

So it's costing more money than what it's worth. But they're wanting to have a copy of like the Gantt chart and things like that. And Brad doesn't want to invest any more time into it unless they're actually fully committed to the job. So can I.

What kind of wording do I need to say in the email? Yes, we're happy to do your works, but before I give you this works, I need your confirmation that you're going to be committed to us and doing the works. Please pay a deposit of like $5,000 so we can progress.
Surely at this rate, at $100,000 job, you have to have a contract for it. Yeah. Yes, yes.

Secondly, if you choose to provide it, you can do so after the contract is signed and the deposit is paid.

You haven't done anything. Sounds to me like he's not an A class client either. Yeah, yeah.

You know, it might look like seven weeks on paper, but it really is.

When we move a kitchen into the lounge room and the lounge room into the kitchen, they don't understand that we have a timeline that we need to work to. If you're still getting pushback and frustration, then maybe he's not the client for you. Yeah, okay, I do agree with you, Miles. I do think that people are getting much, much tighter with their money, and so therefore, they want to be. We tend to be getting more stressed clients.

And so because this is such a big purchase for them and a big investment, they tend to get overly worried about what all the finer details look like. And that's our opportunity to hold that boundary, hold the line.

You know, yes, you may choose to give more explanation than you ordinarily would, but it's gotta be within your boundaries and it's gotta be reciprocated. So whether that be with gratitude or whether it be with the.

The understanding that, okay, that's the boundary, I'm not gonna pass it, or here, I'm gonna do something for you in return, one way or another, you need to see something back for changing your rules. If you do change your rules, and if you're not, then it says to me they're not ideal.
And I'd want you to really consider before you sign on. And Moza's right. It really is the smaller investment. So.

And everybody has a different budget of what a smaller investment is, but they're the ones that, you know, if it feels like a lot of money to somebody, then they. They are ultra tight. They are altered. And it's not. It probably gives a negative connotation, but it is a really big deal to them.

If you think back to our conversation this morning about investing in a Thermomix, for some people, that's a great investment. For other people it's not. But for some people that invest in a Thermomix, they need to get continued value for money out of that or it's not something that is going to work for them.

Whereas for some of us, it's like, well, this is just another tool that I require, and sometimes I'll use it and sometimes I won't. If you're running into people that this is a big investment for them, and so it becomes a big concern for them, they I would call that a red flag. We just need to handle them with kid gloves.

Well, there's a couple of different ways we could run this solution for you.

In your case, if you decide to do it this way, far be it for me to get in the middle of a brand new married couple couple and try and sway you one way or the other. But what I would say to you, I think this is in general for everybody taking holidays. You can absolutely take a holiday.
We just need to set up the structure to support that while you're away. Yes, Josh, I'LL happily take your phone.

And so in that case, that might look like you shipping your phone up to me, or it might look like you organizing a virtual phone answering service or you put some really clear parameters into your message bank.

Now, given that you're a new business, my preference would be a virtual phone answering service who are able to take the information and then pass it on to some subcontractors that you've made agreements with before you go away.

So you remain in control of who gets contact with somebody else at what stage, but you actually don't have to do any of it because you're sipping pina coladas on the beach somewhere overseas or you're in the snow because you're going to Europe.

So a virtual service like the one Amy Jones uses, it's probably the best short term fix here for you can take a message and then they can within some parameters. So you would be responsible for writing up what the parameters look like.
So pardon me, emergency works would be handed out to subcontractor 1 or subcontractor 2, and subcontractor 1 or 2 would be then responsible for following up the client.

If it's non emergency work, you can have another script where they answer and say, Josh is currently away, but he'll be back to give you a call on this date. Are you happy to wait? And if they say no, then we can refer them onto the subcontractor or we can just let them go again.

You might have to do a little bit of troubleshooting and come up with some answers for those questions that they're able to follow then, because it is as simple as them finding exactly what you put in place.

So the only risk that I can see for you in this is that a subcontractor will take on some of the emergency work for you and that may lead to that particular client using that subcontractor moving forward.

However, if you were to weigh up the fact that the initial contact is with you and then you're still creating the solution, and if you've got mates that could do the subby work for you, perhaps they'd be more than happy to agree that you even invoice it out, that could be something that you put in place as well.
Then you've got as much control as you can possibly have while you're away.

The other alternative I think because you're a new business, I wouldn't recommend, but for others on the call, it might work well for you when you go away, and that is to put a message on your message bank and an order responder under your email to let people know that you're away and you'll respond to their inquiry on the day that you come back.

In your case, Josh, because the business is so new, I wouldn't advise that. I think you're right. When it comes to emergency works, we try and give them a solution, we want them to come back to you. And Miles is absolutely right with his comment. Christmas is a great time to go away.
The whole world understands that the trade shut down for a period of two to three weeks over Christmas and they understand it might be hard to get someone.
And if you've got a solution for emergency works, most people are more than happy with that. I'm sorry, I think I cut you off. No, that's okay. I think the services, would that be.
An AI answering thing or a, like a VA assistant?

By the time you find somebody to set it up for you and you do the work to get the scripts right and then you train the bot, you know, it can be quite a complicated process on an ongoing basis, that's fantastic.

But what can work is things like your defects process, information about your licensing and your insurances, if they haven't already asked for it, some information around your team. They often want the security. Knowing that your team is of a size that can handle it.

Those kinds of things can kind of humanize it a tiny, tiny bit. But generally, honestly, even the people that read the tenders then have a set of criteria that they're following. And it's not necessarily.
